records.ety: [OE. chastien, OF. Chastier, F. Ch<?/tier, fr. L. castigare to punish, chastise; castus pure + agere to lead, drive. See Chaste, Act, and cf. Castigate, Chastise.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Chastened (-s'nd); p. pr. & vb. n. Chastening.]
usage: To Chasten, Punish, Chastise. To chasten is to subject to affliction or trouble, in order to produce a general change for the better in life or character. To punish is to inflict penalty for violation of law, disobedience to authority, or intentional wrongdoing. To chastise is to punish a particular offense, as with stripes, especially with the hope that suffering or disgrace may prevent a repetition of faults.

defs: To correct by punishment; to inflict pain upon the purpose of reclaiming; to discipline; as, to chasten a son with a rod.
quotes: For whom the Lord loveth he chasteneth.

defs: To purify from errors or faults; to refine.
quotes: They [classics] chasten and enlarge the mind, and excite to noble actions.
